subject: Physics
q: Four forces are acting at a point P in equilibrium as shown in figure. The ratio of force F1 to F2 is 1:x where x=_____ .
question: Four forces are acting at a point P in equilibrium as shown in figure. The ratio of force F1 to F2 is 1:x where x=_____ .
solution:
Here, θ=45°

Applying equilibrium conditions,

Taking components along x & y

Considering right side positive, ∑Fx=0

F1+1sin45°-2sin45°=0F1=2sin45°-1sin45°

F1=2-12=2-12=12

Taking upward positive,∑Fy=0

1cos45°+2cos45°-F2=0F2=1cos45°+2cos45°

F2=2+12=2+12=32

F1:F2=1:3

x=3

The end of one rod is connected to the floor by a stationary pivot and the end of the other rod has roller that rolls along the floor in a slot. As the roller goes back and forth, a 2 kg weight moves up and down. If the roller is moving towards right at a constant speed, the weight moves up with a :
question: The machine as shown has 2 rods of length 1 m connected by a pivot at the top. The end of one rod is connected to the floor by a stationary pivot and the end of the other rod has roller that rolls along the floor in a slot. As the roller goes back and forth, a 2 kg weight moves up and down. If the roller is moving towards right at a constant speed, the weight moves up with a :
options: Speed which is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> of that of the roller when the weight is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the ground
solution:
vA=dxdt=d2lcosθdt=-2lsinθdθdt and 

Vertical velocity of B, vB=dydt=dlsinθdt=lcosθdθdt

vAvB=-2tanθ⇒vB=-vAcotθ2 
 
θ→increase

cotθ→decrease

Hence, vB→decrease.

subject: Physics
q: A mass of $1\text{ kg}$ is kept on an inclined plane with $30°$ inclination with respect to horizontal plane and it is at rest initially. Then the whole assembly is moved up with constant velocity of $4\text{ m/s}$. The work done by the frictional force in time $2\text{ s}$ is _______ J. (Take $g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
question: A mass of $1\text{ kg}$ is kept on an inclined plane with $30°$ inclination with respect to horizontal plane and it is at rest initially. Then the whole assembly is moved up with constant velocity of $4\text{ m/s}$. The work done by the frictional force in time $2\text{ s}$ is _______ J. (Take $g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
solution:
Since the assembly moves with a constant velocity, the acceleration of the mass is zero. The mass is in equilibrium relative to the incline.

The frictional force $f$ acting on the mass balances the component of gravity along the inclined plane. Therefore, the magnitude of the frictional force is:

$f = mg \sin 30^\circ$

Substituting the given values ($m = 1\text{ kg}$, $g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$):

$f = 1 \times 10 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 5\text{ N}$

The direction of the frictional force is upwards along the incline. Since the incline makes an angle of $30^\circ$ with the horizontal, the frictional force makes an angle of $90^\circ - 30^\circ = 60^\circ$ with the vertical upward direction.

The displacement $s$ of the assembly in time $t = 2\text{ s}$ is vertically upwards:

$s = vt = 4 \times 2 = 8\text{ m}$

The work done by the frictional force is:

$W = f s \cos 60^\circ$

$W = 5 \times 8 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 20\text{ J}$

Answer: $20$

q: Consider a uniform cubical box of side a on a rough floor that is to be moved by applying minimum possible force F at a point b above its centre of mass (see figure). If the coefficient of friction is μ=0.4 , the maximum possible value of 100×ba for a box not to topple before moving is ________
question: Consider a uniform cubical box of side a on a rough floor that is to be moved by applying minimum possible force F at a point b above its centre of mass (see figure). If the coefficient of friction is μ=0.4 , the maximum possible value of 100×ba for a box not to topple before moving is ________
solution:
For no toppling
Fa2+b≤mga2
μa2+μb≤a2
0.2a+0.4b≤0.5a
0.4b≤0.3a
b≤3a2
b≤0.75a (in limiting case)
But it is not possible as b can maximum be equal to 0.5a
∴100bamax=50.00

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is fixed to one end of a light spring having force constant <math><mi>k</mi></math> and unstretched length <math><mi>l</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The other end is fixed. The system is given an angular speed <math><mi>ω</mi></math> about the fixed end of the spring such that it rotates in a circle in gravity free space. Then the stretch in the spring is:
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is fixed to one end of a light spring having force constant <math><mi>k</mi></math> and unstretched length <math><mi>l</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The other end is fixed. The system is given an angular speed <math><mi>ω</mi></math> about the fixed end of the spring such that it rotates in a circle in gravity free space. Then the stretch in the spring is:
solution:
As we know, the spring force will give the necessary centripetal force for rotation.

So, 

mω2l+x=kx
lx+1=kmω2

 Thus, the stretch in the spring
x=lmω2k-mω2

subject: Physics
q: A smooth circular groove has a smooth vertical wall as shown in figure. A block of mass m moves against the wall with a speed v. Which of the following curve represents the correct relation between the normal reaction on the block by the wall N and speed of the block v?
question: A smooth circular groove has a smooth vertical wall as shown in figure. A block of mass m moves against the wall with a speed v. Which of the following curve represents the correct relation between the normal reaction on the block by the wall N and speed of the block v?
solution:
The required centripetal force will be provided by the normal reaction

⇒N=mv2r

This equation is similar to y=kx2 which is a parabola.

subject: Physics
q: A massless spring gets elongated by amount $x_1$ under a tension of 5 N . Its elongation is $x_2$ under the tension of 7 N . For the elongation of $\left(5 x_1-2 x_2\right)$, the tension in the spring will be,
question: A massless spring gets elongated by amount $x_1$ under a tension of 5 N . Its elongation is $x_2$ under the tension of 7 N . For the elongation of $\left(5 x_1-2 x_2\right)$, the tension in the spring will be,
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{kx}_1=5 \mathrm{~N} \\ & \mathrm{kx}_2=7 \mathrm{~N} \\ & \mathrm{k}\left(5 \mathrm{x}_1-2 \mathrm{x}_2\right)=5 \mathrm{kx}_1-2 \mathrm{kx}_2 \\ & =5 \times 5-2 \times 7=11 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

 x=ℓ≈3.5m

now according to work energy theorem

2+mgsin⁡θ=μ mgcos⁡θ

mgsin⁡θ-μ mgcos⁡θ=2 ........1
mgsin⁡θ+ μ mgcos⁡θ=10 .......2

equation (1) devided by (2)

mg sin⁡θ-μcos⁡θmg sin⁡θ+ μcos⁡θ=-210=-15 

1-μ35=-1+μ3

6=43 μ

μ=32

solution: $\mathrm{Kx}=\mathrm{ma}=\frac{\mathrm{mF}}{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{M}}$

subject: Physics
q:
A block is sliding down on an inclined plane of slope $\theta$ and at an instant $t=0$ this block is given an upward momentum so that it starts moving up on the inclined surface with velocity $u$. The distance $(S)$ travelled by the block before its velocity become zero, is $\_\_\_\_$.
($g=$ gravitational acceleration)
question:
A block is sliding down on an inclined plane of slope $\theta$ and at an instant $t=0$ this block is given an upward momentum so that it starts moving up on the inclined surface with velocity $u$. The distance $(S)$ travelled by the block before its velocity become zero, is $\_\_\_\_$.
($g=$ gravitational acceleration)
solution:
$a = -g\sin\theta$
$V^2 = U^2 + 2as$
$0 = u^2 - 2g\sin\theta \cdot s$
$s = \dfrac{u^2}{2g\sin\theta}$

subject: Physics
q: A car of $800 \mathrm{~kg}$ is taking turn on a banked road of radius $300 \mathrm{~m}$ and angle of banking $30^{\circ}$. If coefficient of static friction is 0.2 then the maximum speed with which car can negotiate the turn safely: $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \sqrt{3}=1.73\right)$
question: A car of $800 \mathrm{~kg}$ is taking turn on a banked road of radius $300 \mathrm{~m}$ and angle of banking $30^{\circ}$. If coefficient of static friction is 0.2 then the maximum speed with which car can negotiate the turn safely: $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \sqrt{3}=1.73\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}=800 \mathrm{~kg} \\ & \mathrm{r}=300 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \theta=30^{\circ} \\ & \mu_{\mathrm{s}}=0.2 \\ & \mathrm{~V}_{\max }=\sqrt{\operatorname{Rg}\left[\frac{\tan \theta+\mu}{1-\mu \tan \theta}\right]} \\ & =\sqrt{300 \times \mathrm{g} \times\left[\frac{\tan 30^{\circ}+0.2}{1-0.2 \times \tan 30^{\circ}}\right]} \\ & =\sqrt{300 \times 10 \times\left[\frac{0.57+0.2}{1-0.2 \times 0.57}\right]} \\ & \mathrm{V}_{\max }=51.4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two masses M1 and M2 are tied together at the two ends of a light inextensible string that passes over a frictionless pulley. When the mass M2 is twice that of M1. the acceleration of the system is a1. When the mass M2 is thrice that of M1. The acceleration of The system is a2. The ratio a1a2 will be
question: Two masses M1 and M2 are tied together at the two ends of a light inextensible string that passes over a frictionless pulley. When the mass M2 is twice that of M1. the acceleration of the system is a1. When the mass M2 is thrice that of M1. The acceleration of The system is a2. The ratio a1a2 will be
solution:
In both cases m1>m2. Therefore, m1 will accelerate downward and m2 will accelerate upward.

For m1,

m1g-T=m1a

For m2,

T-m2g=m2a

Adding the above equations we get,

a=m1-m2m1+m2g

⇒a1=m1-m2m1+m2g=2m2-m22m2+m2g=g3

 and

⇒a2=m1-m2m1+m2g=3m2-m23m2+m2g=g2

⇒a1a2=23

Time taken by the ball to rise to its zenith is:

F=-mg-mγv2

dvdt=-g-γv2
∫v=V0v=0dvg+γv2=∫t=0t-dt
1gγtan-1γgvv=V0v=0=-t
∴t=1γgtan-1 γgV0

subject: Physics
q: A $0.5$ kg mass is in contact against the inner wall of a cylindrical drum of radius $4$ m rotating about its vertical axis. The minimum rotational speed of the drum to enable the mass to remain stuck to the wall (without falling) is $5$ rad/s. The coefficient of friction between the drum's inner wall surface and mass is _______. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: A $0.5$ kg mass is in contact against the inner wall of a cylindrical drum of radius $4$ m rotating about its vertical axis. The minimum rotational speed of the drum to enable the mass to remain stuck to the wall (without falling) is $5$ rad/s. The coefficient of friction between the drum's inner wall surface and mass is _______. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
The normal force $N$ provides the necessary centripetal force for the mass to move in a circle:

$N = m \omega^2 R$

For the mass to remain stuck to the wall without falling, the upward frictional force must balance the downward gravitational force:

$f \ge mg$

Since the maximum static friction is $f_{max} = \mu N$, we have:

$\mu N \ge mg$

$\mu (m \omega^2 R) \ge mg$

$\mu \ge \dfrac{g}{\omega^2 R}$

Substituting the given values $g = 10$ m/s$^2$, $\omega = 5$ rad/s, and $R = 4$ m:

$\mu \ge \dfrac{10}{5^2 \times 4}$

$\mu \ge \dfrac{10}{100}$

$\mu \ge 0.1$

The minimum coefficient of friction is $0.1$.

Answer: $0.1$

q:

If the piston is stationary, its mass m will be given by:

(R is universal gas constant and g is the acceleration due to gravity)
question:

If the piston is stationary, its mass m will be given by:

(R is universal gas constant and g is the acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
P1+mgA=P2

⇒nRTl1A+mgA=nRTl2A

⇒m=nRTg1l2-1l1

=nRTgl1-l2l1l2

q: Two masses $m_1=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=4.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ tied to a string are hanging over a light frictionless pulley. What is the acceleration of the masses when lift free to move $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
question: Two masses $m_1=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=4.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ tied to a string are hanging over a light frictionless pulley. What is the acceleration of the masses when lift free to move $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
solution: $\mathrm{a}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}_1-\mathrm{m}_2}{\mathrm{~m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2}\right) \mathrm{g}=0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

subject: Physics
q:
Consider a block kept on an inclined plane (inclined at 45°) as shown in the figure. If the force required to just push it up the incline is 2 times the force required to just prevent it from sliding down, the coefficient of friction between the block and inclined plane (µ) is equal to :

.
question:
Consider a block kept on an inclined plane (inclined at 45°) as shown in the figure. If the force required to just push it up the incline is 2 times the force required to just prevent it from sliding down, the coefficient of friction between the block and inclined plane (µ) is equal to :

.
solution:
The forces acting on block is shown in above figure.

Here, normal force, N=mgcos45° and frictional force, f=μN.

Balancing the force along incline, we have

F1=mg sin45°+f=mg sin45°+μN

⇒F1=mg2+μmg cos45°

⇒F1=mg21+μ

Now, the forces acting on block to just push it up on inclined plane is shown below.

Balancing the force along incline, we have

F2=mg sin45°-f=mg sin45°-μN

=mg21-μ

Given, F1=2F2

⇒mg21+μ=2mg21-μ

⇒1+μ=2-2μ

⇒μ=13=0.33

q: Water is flowing at a speed of 1.5 m s<sup>-1</sup> through a horizontal tube of cross-sectional area 10<sup>-2</sup> m<sup>2</sup><sup> </sup>and you are trying to stop the flow by your palm. Assuming that the water stops immediately after hitting the palm, the minimum force that you must exert should be (density of water = 10<sup>3</sup> kg m<sup>-3</sup>)
question: Water is flowing at a speed of 1.5 m s<sup>-1</sup> through a horizontal tube of cross-sectional area 10<sup>-2</sup> m<sup>2</sup><sup> </sup>and you are trying to stop the flow by your palm. Assuming that the water stops immediately after hitting the palm, the minimum force that you must exert should be (density of water = 10<sup>3</sup> kg m<sup>-3</sup>)
solution:
υ = 1.5 m/s      A = 1 0 - 2 m 2

Now, F = d dt P

            = d dt m υ = υ dm dt

            = A υ · ρ · υ

            = A ρ υ 2

            = 1 0 - 2 × 1 0 0 0 × 1.5 2

            = 1 0 - 2 × 1 0 0 0 × 2.25

            = 22.5 N

q: A given object takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times more time to slide down a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mi>o</mi></msup></math> rough inclined plane as it takes to slide down a perfectly smooth <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> incline. question: A given object takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times more time to slide down a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mi>o</mi></msup></math> rough inclined plane as it takes to slide down a perfectly smooth <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> incline. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the incline is:
solution:
Time taken to slide along a smooth surface is t1.
S=12gsin⁡45ot12
t1=22sg
Time taken to slide along a rough surface is t2.
S=12gsin⁡45o-μgsin⁡45o t22
t2=22sg1-μ
t2=nt1
22sg1-μ=n2×22sg⇒1-μ=1n2⇒μ=1-1n2

q: Two blocks  A and  B of masses mA=1 kg and mB=3 kg are kept on the table as shown in figure. The coefficients of friction between A and B is 0.2 and between  B and the surface of the table is also 0.2. The maximum force F that can be applied on B horizontally, so that the block  A does not slide over the block B is : [Take  g=10 m/s2 ]
solution:
Maximum possible acceleration of 1 kg block a=µmgm=µg=2 m/s2

For 1 g block not to slide the maximum acceleration of both blocks should be

Fmax-µmg=mamax
Fmax-0.2×4×10=4×2
Fmax- 8=8
Fmax=16 N

Fsin30°.

subject: Physics
q: Three forces start acting simultaneously on a particle moving with velocity, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$. These forces are represented in magnitude and direction by the three sides of a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$. The particle will now move with velocity
question: Three forces start acting simultaneously on a particle moving with velocity, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$. These forces are represented in magnitude and direction by the three sides of a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$. The particle will now move with velocity
options: $|\mathrm{v}|$ in the direction of the largest force $\mathrm{BC}$
solution:
According to triangle law of forces, the resultant force is zero.
In presence of zero external force, there is no change in velocity

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass 10 kg is kept on a rough inclined plane as shown in the figure. A force of 3 N is applied on the block. The coefficient of static friction between the plane and the block is 0.6. What should be the minimum value of force P, such that the block does not move downward? (take g=10 ms-2 )
question: A block of mass 10 kg is kept on a rough inclined plane as shown in the figure. A force of 3 N is applied on the block. The coefficient of static friction between the plane and the block is 0.6. What should be the minimum value of force P, such that the block does not move downward? (take g=10 ms-2 )
solution:
Block will be at rest

mgsin⁡θ+3N=P+f

10×10×12+3=P+μmgcos⁡θ

( ∵ To get P minimum friction must be minimum)

Pmin=502+3-0.6×10×10×12

=202+3=32N

subject: Physics
q: One end of a massless rope, which passes over a massless and frictionless pulley $P$ is tied to a hook $C$ while the other end is free. Maximum tension that the rope can bear is $360 \mathrm{~N}$. With what value of maximum safe acceleration (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-2}$ ) can a man of $60 \mathrm{~kg}$ climb on the rope?
question: One end of a massless rope, which passes over a massless and frictionless pulley $P$ is tied to a hook $C$ while the other end is free. Maximum tension that the rope can bear is $360 \mathrm{~N}$. With what value of maximum safe acceleration (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-2}$ ) can a man of $60 \mathrm{~kg}$ climb on the rope?
solution: $\mathrm{T}-60 \mathrm{~g}=60 \mathrm{a} ; \mathrm{T}=3000 \mathrm{~N} ; \quad \therefore \mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
